question.text: Captain Sharma, an Indian Army officer deployed in active warfare near the border, wishes to make a will. He dictates his wishes orally to two fellow soldiers. What type of will is this, and is it valid?

answer.text: This is a Holograph Will and is valid only if entirely in the testator's handwriting
This is a Sham Will and has no testamentary intention, hence invalid
This is an Unprivileged Will and requires two attesting witnesses plus registration
This is a Privileged Will, valid as an oral will made before two witnesses by a soldier in warfare

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A Privileged Will can be made by a soldier in warfare or a mariner at sea. It can be oral (before 2 witnesses) and does not need the formalities of an unprivileged will. Captain Sharma's oral dictation before two soldiers is valid. **The Trap:** A Holograph Will is entirely handwritten by the testator — not oral. A Sham Will has no testamentary intention. Candidates who don't know the "privileged will" category may pick Unprivileged Will. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM X-B tests all types of wills: Conditional, Joint, Mutual, Duplicate, Holograph, Concurrent, Sham, Privileged, and Unprivileged. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** While privileged wills are rare in advisory practice, knowing this concept helps when advising military families. Encourage them to formalize the will when the soldier returns from duty.
